Query: MeZO zeroth-order optimization LoRA adapter parameter efficient fine-tuning memory efficient

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Updating billions of parameters to adapt a pretrained LLM is
prohibitive on consumer hardware; how can we adapt the model
with a small number of additional parameters and match full
fine-tuning performance?


### Proposed method
We propose HyperZero-PEFT, a forward-only, hypernetwork-driven adapter scheme that adapts a frozen LLM using a tiny number of additional parameters while matching full fine-tuning. Insert lightweight, parallel adapters in the FFN (LoRA-style down/up projections) and short learned key/value prefixes in attention. A single shared hypernetwork generates all adapter and prefix weights for every layer from compact inputs that concatenate a task embedding, a layer-id embedding, and a block-type embedding. The generated modules are injected with residual scaling and per-layer learned gates to softly select where adaptation matters most. Only the hypernetwork, small embedding tables, the per-layer gates, and optionally layer norms are trained; the LLM weights remain frozen.

Training is performed with a two-point zeroth-order (ZO) estimator on the hypernetwork parameters, eliminating backpropagation through the base LLM so memory stays near inference. At each step, draw an antithetic perturbation for the hypernetwork weights, run two forward passes to compute a finite-difference loss, and update with an Adam-like optimizer. We deterministically resample perturbations by seed to reduce variance and enable gradient accumulation without extra memory. Because the hypernetwork is small and the base model is frozen, a single consumer GPU (e.g., 8–24 GB) can train billions-parameter LLMs with near-inference memory and throughput.

To maximize accuracy under a tiny parameter budget, we add a short sensitivity warm-up that allocates rank and prefix length where they help most. Using a small held-out set, we probe each layer by perturbing its hyper-embedding and measuring loss change, then allocate higher LoRA rank and/or longer prefixes to the top-k most sensitive layers while shrinking others to minimal or zero rank. The hypernetwork learns to specialize by layer and block type, amortizing parameters across the whole stack and across tasks via the task embedding.

At inference, the hypernetwork generates per-layer adapter/prefix weights on the fly or caches them after the first use; switching tasks reduces to swapping a task embedding. The method is compatible with quantized bases (e.g., 4/8-bit) since only small-side modules are trained and applied in higher precision. With typical settings (LoRA rank 4–16; prefix length 8–16; hypernetwork at 0.2%–0.6% of base), HyperZero-PEFT achieves performance on par with full fine-tuning while training and storing orders of magnitude fewer parameters.

### Rationale
HyperZero-PEFT combines two complementary ideas: (a) a shared hypernetwork that amortizes the cost of generating effective adapters and attention prefixes across all layers and tasks, and (b) forward-only zeroth-order training so adaptation fits on consumer hardware. Prior work shows that hypernetworks conditioned on task/layer/block embeddings can produce both adapter and prefix weights that rival or beat standard PEFT while training a tiny fraction of parameters, suggesting strong capacity-per-parameter. ZO optimization, in turn, matches the memory footprint of inference by avoiding backpropagation, and has been shown to scale to large LLMs on a single 24 GB GPU with competitive convergence.

The sensitivity-based budget allocator exploits the observed low effective curvature rank in LLMs to place rank and prefix length where they matter, letting a very small parameter budget approximate the function class of full fine-tuning. Together, these components deliver near-FT accuracy while training and storing only a small, task-portable hypernetwork and embeddings—precisely the constraints required for consumer devices.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Scaling pretrained language models to many tasks with minimal tunable parameters remains challenging: adapter- and prefix-tuning still introduce sizable per-layer/block parameters and are usually trained per task.
   method:  Use a single shared hypernetwork conditioned on hyper-embeddings that encode task, layer-id, and block-type to generate all weights for both prefix-tuning (HyperPrefix) and adapter-tuning (HyperPELT) across the T5 encoder–decoder. For prefixes, expand the hyper-embedding to the prefix length and project it directly to prefix key/value vectors, eliminating the per-block embedding matrix and MLP of standard prefix-tuning; for adapters, generate the down/up projection weights for adapters inserted in parallel to the FFN with a learnable scaling. Only the hypernetwork, input embeddings (task/layer/block), and layer norms are trained; PLM weights are frozen. On GLUE multi-tasking, this cuts trained parameters per task by about 11× vs Prefix-tuning while improving average performance (e.g., HyperPELT 87.09 with 0.24% trained params/task).

2. [LLM_NLP] DPZero: Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models without Backpropagation (2310.09639)
   problem: In practice, DP first-order fine-tuning adds substantial memory/runtime overhead (e.g., per-sample gradient clipping), limiting DP adaptation of very large LLMs on commodity GPUs.
   method:  DPZero performs per-sample clipping on a scalar finite-difference computed with forward passes and injects scalar Gaussian noise, avoiding backprop and per-sample gradient computations (Remark 4.5, lines 205, 209). Experiments show near-zero extra memory/time versus non-private MeZO when fine-tuning RoBERTa (e.g., 2668 MiB and 0.347 s/iter vs MeZO’s 2668 MiB and 0.345 s/iter; DP-AdamW uses 17126 MiB and 2.12 s/iter; Table 3, lines 249–259) and scalability to OPT-6.7B on a single 24 GiB GPU where first-order methods OOM (Table 11, lines 582–589). Utility drops are small under DP (e.g., ≤0.9% at ε=6 on OPT tasks; lines 262–284), validating practical scalability with strong privacy.

3. [LLM_NLP] On the Convergence of Zeroth-Order Federated Tuning for Large Language Models (2402.05926)
   problem: Federated fine-tuning of large language models on resource-limited clients is hindered by the high memory cost of backpropagation and heavy communication, and the convergence behavior of zeroth-order optimization in federated LLM tuning is not established.
   method:  Propose FedMeZO, which integrates the MeZO two-point zeroth-order gradient estimator into FedAvg to eliminate backpropagation, resampling perturbations in-place via random seeds so training memory is comparable to inference, and uses LoRA to update/upload only low-rank adapters to cut communication. The paper derives convergence guarantees that exploit the low effective Hessian rank r of LLMs, proving global rates O(r^{3/2}(NHT)^{-1/2}) in i.i.d. data and O(r^{3/2}((c_h+N)NHT)^{-1/2}) minus a heterogeneity term in non-i.i.d. settings, showing dependence on r rather than the full parameter dimension d. Experiments on LLaMA-based tuning confirm faster loss reduction than BP-based FedAvg with roughly half the GPU memory footprint.

4. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Equipping text-pretrained LMs with vision-and-language capabilities efficiently—without appending image tokens that consume input length or full-model fine-tuning—requires a new mechanism to inject visual information with few parameters.
   method:  VL-HyperPELT treats image features as inputs to hypernetworks rather than as sequence tokens: CLIP-extracted visual features are projected into visual hyper-embeddings, which drive visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific prefix vectors for attention and adapter weights for FFN. These are combined with task-specific outputs (concatenating prefix vectors; parallel adapters) to modulate the frozen T5 while preserving the original text input length. This approach achieves comparable or better performance than VL-Adapter with fewer trainable parameters (6.62% vs 7.16%) and yields stronger few-shot transfer on OKVQA and SNLI-VE.

5. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Playing Lottery Tickets with Vision and Language (2104.11832)
   problem: Large vision-and-language (VLP) models (e.g., UNITER) are over-parameterized, making them costly to deploy; it is unknown whether sparse, trainable subnetworks (lottery tickets) exist that retain performance across diverse VL tasks.
   method:  Conduct the first empirical study of the lottery ticket hypothesis in VLP by applying Iterative Magnitude-based Pruning (IMP) to pre-trained UNITER and re-training from the pre-trained initialization. Across 7 VL tasks (VQA, GQA, VCR, NLVR2, VE, RefCOCO+, Flickr30k IR/TR), identify 'relaxed' winning tickets that maintain ≥99% of full-model accuracy at 50–70% sparsity (e.g., VQA/GQA/RefCOCO+ at 70%, NLVR2/VE/ITR at 60%, VCR at 50%). Show that learned masks are crucial (random pruning underperforms) and that initialization matters (BERT init < UNITER init; shuffled init fails), while rewinding offers little benefit in this setting.

7. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

8. [MULTIMODAL] Bridging Vision and Language Encoders: Parameter-Efficient Tuning for Referring Image Segmentation (2307.11545)
   problem: Referring image segmentation with pre-trained dual-encoder vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) needs strong cross-modal interaction and spatial inductive biases, but full fine-tuning is costly; keeping backbones frozen typically hurts performance.
   method:  Introduce Bridger, an adapter-like module inserted at intermediate stages of frozen image and text encoders to enable early, bidirectional interaction while injecting vision-specific inductive biases. Bridger comprises: (1) a Zoom Layer that reshapes visual features with convolution/deconvolution (for ViT and ResNet) to capture local spatial priors and control sequence length; and (2) an Interactor that performs cross-modal attention using one modality as query and the other as keys/values, then projects and residually adds outputs back into the next backbone stage. This design updates only 1.61%–3.38% of backbone parameters yet matches or surpasses full fine-tuning; ablations show removing Bridger drops oIoU by ~3.33%.

9. [CV] Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models (2401.08541)
   problem: Next-patch pixel prediction can force the backbone to specialize in low-level details, hurting the quality of transferable features.
   method:  Insert a heavily-parameterized token-level MLP prediction head atop the ViT trunk that processes each patch independently before projecting to pixels. This head captures low-level signals needed for accurate pixel regression, keeping the trunk focused on higher-level, transferable representations. Experiments show the MLP head significantly improves downstream performance over having no head, a transformer head brings only marginal gains at much higher cost, and increasing head depth (more MLP blocks) is especially beneficial.

10. [IR_REC] Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems (2208.04560)
   problem: Evaluating new long-term optimization policies offline is risky because standard OPE can overestimate returns on out-of-distribution state-action pairs, potentially promoting harmful policies to online A/B tests.
   method:  Propose a Conservative Offline Policy Estimator that performs fitted Q evaluation augmented with a CQL-style regularizer to penalize Q-values for OOD actions, producing a lower-bound estimate of a policy’s per-session long-term value from held-out logs. This estimator guides offline model selection and aligns with observed online outcomes, filtering policies that would degrade user experience.

11. [RL] Reward-Weighted Regression Converges to a Global Optimum (2107.09088)
   problem: EM-based Reward-Weighted Regression (RWR) is known to provide monotonic policy improvements under some conditions, but it lacked a clear, analyzable policy update in terms of value functions and precise conditions guaranteeing improvement without relying on potentially distorting nonlinear reward transformations.
   method:  Derive a closed-form RWR policy update (no function approximation) expressed via value functions: define B(π) with π_{n+1}(a|s) = (Q^{π_n}(s,a)/V^{π_n}(s)) · π_n(a|s). Prove this induces monotonic improvement of both V^{π} and Q^{π} at every state-action (Theorem 4.1), with strict improvement whenever Var_{a∼π_n(·|s)}[Q^{π_n}(s,a)]>0. Show that nonlinear reward transformations can change the optimal policy (Appendix A), so restrict to linear shifts yielding strictly positive rewards to ground the update and analysis (Theorem 3.1).

12. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] Continuous Input Embedding Size Search For Recommender Systems (2304.03501)
   problem: During RL search, the base recommender is only partially trained per iteration, so a single embedding-size decision can be brittle; practitioners also need precise sparsity targets and masks for multiple budgets without rerunning search.
   method:  CIESS maintains a top-l pool of candidate embedding masks that meet the target sparsity based on their quality indicators during search. After search, it performs selective retraining to convergence for each candidate and selects the best-performing mask; the scheme can be extended to additional lower sparsities to obtain optimal masks for multiple memory budgets in one run.

15. [IR_REC] ERANK: Fusing Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning for Effective and Efficient Text Reranking (2509.00520)
   problem: Efficient pointwise rerankers lack global listwise awareness, while listwise/setwise RL training is effective but incurs prohibitive latency and memory for real-world deployment.
   method:  Adopt a two-stage pipeline that adds RL with GRPO to an SFT-tuned pointwise model, using a novel listwise-derived, rule-based reward r_RR. For each query, aggregate N×G scores from all documents and rollouts, sort them, and compute rewards that: (a) give positives high reciprocal-rank rewards, (b) penalize negatives ranked above any positive, and (c) give correctly-below negatives a smooth squared-error reward against the SFT reference score. Use KL regularization to stabilize policy updates. This injects global ranking signals into a pointwise architecture while preserving low-latency inference.

16. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
   method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

17. [SPEECH] Application-Agnostic Language Modeling for On-Device ASR (2305.09764)
   problem: On-device ASR must serve both virtual-assistant (VA) and speech-to-text (STT) use cases with very different linguistic distributions, but naively training a single LM on merged data degrades accuracy; training per-application LMs meets accuracy but doubles disk/memory.
   method:  They propose a data balancing scheme that derives per-source sampling weights for a single, application-agnostic NNLM. First, build a balanced dev set (≈equal VA/STT). Compute optimal interpolation weights that minimize perplexity for application-specific LMs on this dev set to obtain redistribution masses for VA and STT. Use these to rescale initial per-source sampling weights (from count-merging) and renormalize, then sample heldout/train data accordingly. This yields a unified training mix that benefits both applications (Sec. 3, lines 59–71).

18. [SPEECH] Branchformer: Parallel MLP-Attention Architectures to Capture Local and Global Context for Speech Recognition and Understanding (2207.02971)
   problem: End-to-end ASR/SLU encoders must capture both local and global dependencies, but popular designs like Conformer combine convolution and self-attention sequentially, making utilization of local/global cues hard to interpret or customize and leaving quadratic attention cost.
   method:  Branchformer introduces a two-branch encoder where one branch uses multi-headed self-attention with relative positional encoding for global context, and the other uses an MLP with convolutional spatial gating (cgMLP) employing depth-wise convolutions for local context. The branches are merged via either concatenation (default, best accuracy) or a learned weighted average whose attention-pooled weights expose layer-wise importance of local vs. global processing. This parallel, disentangled design yields better or comparable results to Conformer across ASR/SLU benchmarks and improves training stability on short-utterance data.

19. [ROBOTICS] Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation (1909.12906)
   problem: On-policy adaptation on real robots is sample-inefficient and risky in high-dimensional, low-level action spaces, leading to unsafe or erratic exploration.
   method:  Train a variational autoencoder on a dataset of safe, task-specific robot trajectories to define a low-dimensional latent action space; the policy outputs latent vectors conditioned on the goal, which are decoded into smooth executable trajectories. This reduces action dimensionality, focuses learning on terminal rewards, and enables faster, safer on-policy adaptation. The learned 2D latent space disentangles control factors (e.g., hitting angle vs. strength), yielding predictable control and stable updates.

20. [ROBOTICS] Causal Reasoning in Simulation for Structure and Transfer Learning of Robot Manipulation Policies (2103.16772)
   problem: Sim-to-real manipulation policies often overfit to large, distractor-laden state spaces, making them brittle under domain shifts and sample-inefficient to adapt. A key unmet need is to identify which context/state variables are causally relevant to a parameterized controller and to exploit this structure for transfer.
   method:  CREST performs simulation-based causal reasoning with an internal (approximate) model to discover the policy’s input structure. Phase 1 intervenes on one context variable at a time (||Δc||0=1) after solving for task-specific controller parameters θi, and marks a variable as relevant if reusing θi fails; this yields the relevant set τ. Phase 2 identifies parameter-specific inputs τj by re-solving under interventions on τ and using a breadth-first search to find a minimal set of parameter changes (min ||Δθ||0), assigning each θj the variables whose interventions necessitated changing θj. The learned structure is encoded in Reduced MLPs (inputs τ) and Partitioned MLPs (per-parameter towers with inputs τj), pretrained in the internal model with domain randomization only over τ, then transferred and fine-tuned in the target domain.

21. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

1. Evolution of meta's llama models and parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models: a survey (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)
   This review surveys the rapid evolution of Meta AI's LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) series - from LLaMA 1 through LLaMA 4 and the specialized parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods developed for these models. We first describe the LLaMA family of foundation models (7B-65B to 288B parameters), their architectures (including native multimodal and Mixtureof-Experts variants), and key performance characteristics. We then describe and discuss the concept of PEFT, which adapts large pre-trained models by updating only a small subset of parameters, and review five PEFT methods that have been applied to LLaMA: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), LLaMA-Adapter V1 and V2, LLaMA-Excitor, and QLoRA (Quantized LoRA). We discuss each method's mechanism, parameter savings, and example application to LLaMA (e.g., instruction tuning, multimodal tasks). We provide structured discussion and analysis of model and adapter architectures, parameter counts, and benchmark results (including examples where fine-tuned LLaMA models outperform larger baselines). Finally, we examine real-world use cases where LLaMA-based models and PEFT have been successfully applied (e.g., legal and medical domains), and we discuss ongoing challenges and future research directions (such as scaling to even larger contexts and improving robustness). This survey paper provides a one-stop resource for ML researchers and practitioners interested in LLaMA models and efficient fine-tuning strategies.

2. MoA: Heterogeneous Mixture of Adapters for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Recent studies integrate Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to further enhance the performance of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods in Large Language Model (LLM) applications. Existing methods employ \emph{homogeneous} MoE-LoRA architectures composed of LoRA experts with either similar or identical structures and capacities. However, these approaches often suffer from representation collapse and expert load imbalance, which negatively impact the potential of LLMs. To address these challenges, we propose a \emph{heterogeneous} \textbf{Mixture-of-Adapters (MoA)} approach. This method dynamically integrates PEFT adapter experts with diverse structures, leveraging their complementary representational capabilities to foster expert specialization, thereby enhancing the effective transfer of pre-trained knowledge to downstream tasks. MoA supports two variants: \textbf{(i)} \textit{Soft MoA} achieves fine-grained integration by performing a weighted fusion of all expert outputs; \textbf{(ii)} \textit{Sparse MoA} activates adapter experts sparsely based on their contribution, achieving this with negligible performance degradation. Experimental results demonstrate that heterogeneous MoA outperforms homogeneous MoE-LoRA methods in both performance and parameter efficiency. Our project is available at https://github.com/DCDmllm/MoA.

3. HSplitLoRA: A Heterogeneous Split Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Framework for Large Language Models (IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing 2025, cites: 28)
   Recently, large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable breakthroughs, revolutionizing the natural language processing domain and beyond. Due to immense parameter sizes, fine-tuning these models with private data for diverse downstream tasks has become mainstream. Though federated learning (FL) offers a promising solution for fine-tuning LLMs without sharing raw data, substantial computing costs hinder its democratization. Moreover, in real-world scenarios, private client devices often possess heterogeneous computing resources, further complicating LLM fine-tuning. To combat these challenges, we propose HSplitLoRA, a heterogeneous parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) framework built on split learning (SL) and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning, for efficiently fine-tuning LLMs on heterogeneous client devices. HSplitLoRA first identifies important weights based on their contributions to LLM training. It then dynamically configures the decomposition ranks of LoRA adapters for selected weights and determines the model split point according to varying computing budgets of client devices. Finally, a noise-free adapter aggregation mechanism is devised to support heterogeneous adapter aggregation without introducing noise. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HSplitLoRA outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks in training accuracy and convergence speed.

   Deploying large language models (LLMs) with their extensive parameters and high memory demands challenges computational efficiency, particularly in fine-tuning for specific applications with limited resources. Techniques like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) help by training a smaller, modifiable extension of the base model to reduce memory usage. However, combining quantization with LoRA, especially in low-bit scenarios, can lead to performance losses due to quantization errors. Our innovative Rank-Adaptive LoRA (RA-LoRA) addresses this by dynamically adjusting the adapter’s rank us-ing rank-subspace analysis, optimizing performance with fewer parameters. We tested RA-LoRA on state-of-the-art LLMs for 2-bit efficient fine-tuning, showing it can improve model accuracy with minimal trainable parameters, marking a leap forward in quantization-aware fine-tuning methods and highlighting the significance of rank dynamics in optimizing quantized LLMs.

Due to their massive scale, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods are widely used to reduce training costs. Among them, hybrid PEFT methods that combine multiple PEFT techniques have achieved the best performance. However, existing hybrid PEFT methods face two main challenges when fine-tuning LLMs for specialized applications: (1) relying on point estimates, lacking the ability to quantify uncertainty for reliable decision-making, and (2) struggling to dynamically adapt to emerging data, lacking the ability to suit real-world situations. We propose Bayesian Hybrid Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (BH-PEFT), a novel method that integrates Bayesian learning into hybrid PEFT. BH-PEFT combines Adapter, LoRA, and prefix-tuning to fine-tune feedforward and attention layers of the Transformer. By modeling learnable parameters as distributions, BH-PEFT enables uncertainty quantification. We further propose a Bayesian dynamic fine-tuning approach where the last posterior serves as the prior for the next round, enabling effective adaptation to new data. We evaluated BH-PEFT on business tasks such as sentiment analysis, news categorization, and commonsense reasoning. Results show that our method outperforms existing PEFT baselines, enables uncertainty quantification for more reliable decisions, and improves adaptability in dynamic scenarios. This work contributes to business analytics and data science by proposing a novel BH-PEFT method and dynamic fine-tuning approach that support uncertainty-aware and adaptive decision-making in real-world situations.

Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is well-known for its parameter efficiency. It can reduce the number of parameters needed to fine-tune LLMs by several orders of magnitude. However, LoRA-based approaches encounter a significant limitation due to the bottleneck imposed by rank one decomposition. As the parameters count in LLMs increase, even rank one decomposition might surpass the number of parameters truly necessary for handling more downstream tasks. In this paper, we propose a new method for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) via deconvolution in subspace, dubbed as DCFT. We innovatively use deconvolution to complete details and enhance knowledge in subspace incremental matrices, and dynamically control parameters by adjusting the kernel size, unconstrained by rank-one decomposition. Extensive experiments are conducted to validate the effectiveness of DCFT. Results show that compared to LoRA, DCFT achieve an 8$\times$ reduction in parameters, and still achieves highly impressive performance. Our code is available here: https://github.com/Godz-z/DCFT.

This allows the model to adjust its structure flexibly in multi-task settings to match different task characteristics. With the backbone parameters kept frozen, the method uses a structure search mechanism to guide the dynamic construction of task-specific efficient substructures during training. This significantly improves parameter utilization and representational capacity. In addition, the paper designs a set of sensitivity analysis experiments to systematically evaluate the effects of sparsity weight, noise injection ratio, and data perturbation on model performance. These experiments verify the stability and robustness of the proposed method across various multi-task natural language understanding tasks. The experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms mainstream parameter-efficient tuning techniques on multiple tasks. It achieves a better balance among accuracy, compression rate, and robustness to noise and perturbation.

8. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models for Unit Test Generation: An Empirical Study (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 10)
   Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, which fine-tune only a subset of model parameters, offer a promising solution by reducing the computational costs of tuning large language models (LLMs) while maintaining their performance. Existing studies have explored using PEFT and LLMs for various code-related tasks and found that the effectiveness of PEFT techniques is task-dependent. The state-of-the-art is limited to using LLMs with full fine-tuning to generate unit tests. The application of PEFT techniques in unit test generation remains underexplored. This paper investigates both full fine-tuning and various PEFT methods, including LoRA, (IA)^3, and prompt tuning, across thirteen models of different architectures and sizes. We use well-established benchmark datasets to evaluate their effectiveness in unit test generation and measure syntax correctness, CodeBLEU, pass@1, instruction coverage, branch coverage, and mutation score of the generated tests. Our findings show that LoRA can deliver performance comparable to full fine-tuning for unit test generation in several cases. If training costs are valued, prompt tuning is the most cost-effective approach, particularly for large models. However, the models tuned with full fine-tuning or PEFT may generate fewer executable test cases than the baseline model because they generate more tests calling nonexistent methods or having type mismatches. For the generated ones that are executable, the ones from the tuned models show better test coverage than those from the baseline model.

9. Harnessing the Power of Single-Cell Large Language Models with Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning using scPEFT (bioRxiv 2025, cites: 2)
   Single-cell large language models (scLLMs) capture essential biological insights from vast single-cell atlases but struggle in out-of-context applications, where zero-shot predictions can be unreliable. To address this, we introduce a single-cell parameter-efficient fine-tuning (scPEFT) framework that integrates learnable, low-dimensional adapters into scLLMs. By freezing the backbone model and updating only the adapter parameters, scPEFT efficiently adapts to specific tasks using limited custom data. This approach mitigates catastrophic forgetting, reduces parameter tuning by over 96%, and decreases GPU memory usage by more than half, significantly enhancing scLLMs’s accessibility for resource-constrained researchers. Validated across diverse datasets, scPEFT outperformed zero-shot models and traditional fine-tuning in disease-specific, cross-species, and under-characterized cell population tasks. Its attention-mechanism analysis identified COVID-related genes associated with specific cell states and uncovered unique blood cell subpopulations, demonstrating scPEFT’s capacity for condition-specific interpretations. These findings position scPEFT as an efficient solution for improving scLLMs’ utilities in general single-cell analyses.

10. QuIC: Quantum-Inspired Compound Adapters for Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning ( 2025, cites: 3)
   Scaling full finetuning of large foundation models strains GPU memory and training time. Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods address this issue via adapter modules which update only a small subset of model parameters. In this work, we introduce Quantum-Inspired Compound Adapters (QuIC Adapters), a PEFT approach inspired from Hamming-weight preserving quantum circuits that can effectively finetune a model using less than 0.02\% memory footprint of the base model. QuIC adapters preserve pretrained representations by enforcing orthogonality in weight parameters, and have native deployment mechanisms on quantum computers. We test QuIC adapters by finetuning large language models like LLaMA and vision transformers on language, math, reasoning and vision benchmarks. In its first-order configuration, QuIC recovers the performance of existing orthogonal methods, while higher-order configurations enable substantial parameter compression (over 40x smaller than LoRA) for a modest performance trade-off, unlocking applications in highly resource-constrained environments. Through ablation studies, we determine that combining multiple Hamming-weight orders with orthogonality and matrix compounding are essential for performant finetuning. Our findings suggest that QuIC adapters offers a promising direction for efficient finetuning of foundation models in resource-constrained environments.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Updating billions of parameters to adapt a pretrained LLM is
prohibitive on consumer hardware; how can we adapt the model
with a small number of additional parameters and match full
fine-tuning performance?


### Proposed method
We propose HyperZero-PEFT, a forward-only, hypernetwork-driven adapter scheme that adapts a frozen LLM using a tiny number of additional parameters while matching full fine-tuning. Insert lightweight, parallel adapters in the FFN (LoRA-style down/up projections) and short learned key/value prefixes in attention. A single shared hypernetwork generates all adapter and prefix weights for every layer from compact inputs that concatenate a task embedding, a layer-id embedding, and a block-type embedding. The generated modules are injected with residual scaling and per-layer learned gates to softly select where adaptation matters most. Only the hypernetwork, small embedding tables, the per-layer gates, and optionally layer norms are trained; the LLM weights remain frozen.

Training is performed with a two-point zeroth-order (ZO) estimator on the hypernetwork parameters, eliminating backpropagation through the base LLM so memory stays near inference. At each step, draw an antithetic perturbation for the hypernetwork weights, run two forward passes to compute a finite-difference loss, and update with an Adam-like optimizer. We deterministically resample perturbations by seed to reduce variance and enable gradient accumulation without extra memory. Because the hypernetwork is small and the base model is frozen, a single consumer GPU (e.g., 8–24 GB) can train billions-parameter LLMs with near-inference memory and throughput.

To maximize accuracy under a tiny parameter budget, we add a short sensitivity warm-up that allocates rank and prefix length where they help most. Using a small held-out set, we probe each layer by perturbing its hyper-embedding and measuring loss change, then allocate higher LoRA rank and/or longer prefixes to the top-k most sensitive layers while shrinking others to minimal or zero rank. The hypernetwork learns to specialize by layer and block type, amortizing parameters across the whole stack and across tasks via the task embedding.

At inference, the hypernetwork generates per-layer adapter/prefix weights on the fly or caches them after the first use; switching tasks reduces to swapping a task embedding. The method is compatible with quantized bases (e.g., 4/8-bit) since only small-side modules are trained and applied in higher precision. With typical settings (LoRA rank 4–16; prefix length 8–16; hypernetwork at 0.2%–0.6% of base), HyperZero-PEFT achieves performance on par with full fine-tuning while training and storing orders of magnitude fewer parameters.

### Rationale
HyperZero-PEFT combines two complementary ideas: (a) a shared hypernetwork that amortizes the cost of generating effective adapters and attention prefixes across all layers and tasks, and (b) forward-only zeroth-order training so adaptation fits on consumer hardware. Prior work shows that hypernetworks conditioned on task/layer/block embeddings can produce both adapter and prefix weights that rival or beat standard PEFT while training a tiny fraction of parameters, suggesting strong capacity-per-parameter. ZO optimization, in turn, matches the memory footprint of inference by avoiding backpropagation, and has been shown to scale to large LLMs on a single 24 GB GPU with competitive convergence.

The sensitivity-based budget allocator exploits the observed low effective curvature rank in LLMs to place rank and prefix length where they matter, letting a very small parameter budget approximate the function class of full fine-tuning. Together, these components deliver near-FT accuracy while training and storing only a small, task-portable hypernetwork and embeddings—precisely the constraints required for consumer devices.

   method:  Use a single shared hypernetwork conditioned on hyper-embeddings that encode task, layer-id, and block-type to generate all weights for both prefix-tuning (HyperPrefix) and adapter-tuning (HyperPELT) across the T5 encoder–decoder. For prefixes, expand the hyper-embedding to the prefix length and project it directly to prefix key/value vectors, eliminating the per-block embedding matrix and MLP of standard prefix-tuning; for adapters, generate the down/up projection weights for adapters inserted in parallel to the FFN with a learnable scaling. Only the hypernetwork, input embeddings (task/layer/block), and layer norms are trained; PLM weights are frozen. On GLUE multi-tasking, this cuts trained parameters per task by about 11× vs Prefix-tuning while improving average performance (e.g., HyperPELT 87.09 with 0.24% trained params/task).

   problem: In practice, DP first-order fine-tuning adds substantial memory/runtime overhead (e.g., per-sample gradient clipping), limiting DP adaptation of very large LLMs on commodity GPUs.
   method:  DPZero performs per-sample clipping on a scalar finite-difference computed with forward passes and injects scalar Gaussian noise, avoiding backprop and per-sample gradient computations (Remark 4.5, lines 205, 209). Experiments show near-zero extra memory/time versus non-private MeZO when fine-tuning RoBERTa (e.g., 2668 MiB and 0.347 s/iter vs MeZO’s 2668 MiB and 0.345 s/iter; DP-AdamW uses 17126 MiB and 2.12 s/iter; Table 3, lines 249–259) and scalability to OPT-6.7B on a single 24 GiB GPU where first-order methods OOM (Table 11, lines 582–589). Utility drops are small under DP (e.g., ≤0.9% at ε=6 on OPT tasks; lines 262–284), validating practical scalability with strong privacy.

The paper derives convergence guarantees that exploit the low effective Hessian rank r of LLMs, proving global rates O(r^{3/2}(NHT)^{-1/2}) in i.i.d. data and O(r^{3/2}((c_h+N)NHT)^{-1/2}) minus a heterogeneity term in non-i.i.d. settings, showing dependence on r rather than the full parameter dimension d. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

Across 7 VL tasks (VQA, GQA, VCR, NLVR2, VE, RefCOCO+, Flickr30k IR/TR), identify 'relaxed' winning tickets that maintain ≥99% of full-model accuracy at 50–70% sparsity (e.g., VQA/GQA/RefCOCO+ at 70%, NLVR2/VE/ITR at 60%, VCR at 50%).    problem: Sim-to-real manipulation policies often overfit to large, distractor-laden state spaces, making them brittle under domain shifts and sample-inefficient to adapt. A key unmet need is to identify which context/state variables are causally relevant to a parameterized controller and to exploit this structure for transfer.
   method:  CREST performs simulation-based causal reasoning with an internal (approximate) model to discover the policy’s input structure. Phase 1 intervenes on one context variable at a time (||Δc||0=1) after solving for task-specific controller parameters θi, and marks a variable as relevant if reusing θi fails; this yields the relevant set τ. Phase 2 identifies parameter-specific inputs τj by re-solving under interventions on τ and using a breadth-first search to find a minimal set of parameter changes (min ||Δθ||0), assigning each θj the variables whose interventions necessitated changing θj. The learned structure is encoded in Reduced MLPs (inputs τ) and Partitioned MLPs (per-parameter towers with inputs τj), pretrained in the internal model with domain randomization only over τ, then transferred and fine-tuned in the target domain.

The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

1. Evolution of meta's llama models and parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models: a survey (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)
   This review surveys the rapid evolution of Meta AI's LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) series - from LLaMA 1 through LLaMA 4 and the specialized parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods developed for these models. We first describe the LLaMA family of foundation models (7B-65B to 288B parameters), their architectures (including native multimodal and Mixtureof-Experts variants), and key performance characteristics. We then describe and discuss the concept of PEFT, which adapts large pre-trained models by updating only a small subset of parameters, and review five PEFT methods that have been applied to LLaMA: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), LLaMA-Adapter V1 and V2, LLaMA-Excitor, and QLoRA (Quantized LoRA). We discuss each method's mechanism, parameter savings, and example application to LLaMA (e.g., instruction tuning, multimodal tasks). We provide structured discussion and analysis of model and adapter architectures, parameter counts, and benchmark results (including examples where fine-tuned LLaMA models outperform larger baselines). Finally, we examine real-world use cases where LLaMA-based models and PEFT have been successfully applied (e.g., legal and medical domains), and we discuss ongoing challenges and future research directions (such as scaling to even larger contexts and improving robustness). This survey paper provides a one-stop resource for ML researchers and practitioners interested in LLaMA models and efficient fine-tuning strategies.

2. MoA: Heterogeneous Mixture of Adapters for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Recent studies integrate Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to further enhance the performance of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods in Large Language Model (LLM) applications. Existing methods employ \emph{homogeneous} MoE-LoRA architectures composed of LoRA experts with either similar or identical structures and capacities. However, these approaches often suffer from representation collapse and expert load imbalance, which negatively impact the potential of LLMs. To address these challenges, we propose a \emph{heterogeneous} \textbf{Mixture-of-Adapters (MoA)} approach. This method dynamically integrates PEFT adapter experts with diverse structures, leveraging their complementary representational capabilities to foster expert specialization, thereby enhancing the effective transfer of pre-trained knowledge to downstream tasks. MoA supports two variants: \textbf{(i)} \textit{Soft MoA} achieves fine-grained integration by performing a weighted fusion of all expert outputs; \textbf{(ii)} \textit{Sparse MoA} activates adapter experts sparsely based on their contribution, achieving this with negligible performance degradation. Experimental results demonstrate that heterogeneous MoA outperforms homogeneous MoE-LoRA methods in both performance and parameter efficiency. Our project is available at https://github.com/DCDmllm/MoA.

3. HSplitLoRA: A Heterogeneous Split Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Framework for Large Language Models (IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing 2025, cites: 28)
   Recently, large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable breakthroughs, revolutionizing the natural language processing domain and beyond. Due to immense parameter sizes, fine-tuning these models with private data for diverse downstream tasks has become mainstream. Though federated learning (FL) offers a promising solution for fine-tuning LLMs without sharing raw data, substantial computing costs hinder its democratization. Moreover, in real-world scenarios, private client devices often possess heterogeneous computing resources, further complicating LLM fine-tuning. To combat these challenges, we propose HSplitLoRA, a heterogeneous parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) framework built on split learning (SL) and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning, for efficiently fine-tuning LLMs on heterogeneous client devices. HSplitLoRA first identifies important weights based on their contributions to LLM training. It then dynamically configures the decomposition ranks of LoRA adapters for selected weights and determines the model split point according to varying computing budgets of client devices. Finally, a noise-free adapter aggregation mechanism is devised to support heterogeneous adapter aggregation without introducing noise. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HSplitLoRA outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks in training accuracy and convergence speed.

4. RA-LoRA: Rank-Adaptive Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning for Accurate 2-bit Quantized Large Language Models (Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics 2024, cites: 15)
   Deploying large language models (LLMs) with their extensive parameters and high memory demands challenges computational efficiency, particularly in fine-tuning for specific applications with limited resources. Techniques like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) help by training a smaller, modifiable extension of the base model to reduce memory usage. However, combining quantization with LoRA, especially in low-bit scenarios, can lead to performance losses due to quantization errors. Our innovative Rank-Adaptive LoRA (RA-LoRA) addresses this by dynamically adjusting the adapter’s rank us-ing rank-subspace analysis, optimizing performance with fewer parameters. We tested RA-LoRA on state-of-the-art LLMs for 2-bit efficient fine-tuning, showing it can improve model accuracy with minimal trainable parameters, marking a leap forward in quantization-aware fine-tuning methods and highlighting the significance of rank dynamics in optimizing quantized LLMs.

5. A Bayesian Hybrid Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Method for Large Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated transformative potential in reshaping the world. As these models are pretrained on general corpora, they often require domain-specific fine-tuning to optimize performance in specialized business applications. Due to their massive scale, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods are widely used to reduce training costs. Among them, hybrid PEFT methods that combine multiple PEFT techniques have achieved the best performance. However, existing hybrid PEFT methods face two main challenges when fine-tuning LLMs for specialized applications: (1) relying on point estimates, lacking the ability to quantify uncertainty for reliable decision-making, and (2) struggling to dynamically adapt to emerging data, lacking the ability to suit real-world situations.    This paper studies the challenging task of Referring Expression Comprehension (REC), which aims at detecting the text-referred target object in an input image. However, all these methods process pretrained features in a position-agnostic manner. This will limit their effectiveness in REC tasks, where the positional information is essential to correctly localize the target object. To this end, we propose a novel parameter-efficient tuning approach, named Multi-Modal Adaptive Positional Encoding (MAP), which addresses the above problem from a new perspective of positional encoding. More specifically, MAP first generates initial positional embeddings for different visual encoder layers from a set of learnable vectors, and then adjusts them adaptively based on spatial-wise visual-linguistic correlations of input data. In this way, the positional information of different image tokens can be appropriately modeled and utilized by MAP, thus making it more applicable to REC tasks. Extensive experiments on five widely-used datasets demonstrate that MAP achieves comparable results to full fine-tuning methods with much fewer extra parameters and outperforms other parameter-efficient tuning approaches. 10. Adapter Tuning With Task-Aware Attention Mechanism (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing 2023, cites: 1)
   Adapter-tuning inserts simple feed-forward layers (adapters) in pre-trained language models (PLMs) and just tunes the adapters when transferring to downstream tasks, having become the state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning (PET) strategy. Although the adapters aim to learn task-related representations, their inputs are still obtained from the task-independent and frozen multi-head attention (MHA) modules, leading to insufficient utilization of contextual information for various downstream tasks. Intuitively, MHA should be task-dependent and could attend to different contexts in different downstream tasks. Thus, this paper proposes the task-aware attention mechanism (TAM) to enhance adapter tuning. Specifically, we first utilize the task-dependent adapter to generate token-wise task embedding. Then, we apply the task embedding to influence MHA which task-dependently aggregates the contextual information. Experimental results on a wide range of natural language understanding and generation tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Furthermore, extensive analyses demonstrate that the generated task embedding corresponds with the difficulty of tasks.

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).
