## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Updating billions of parameters to adapt a pretrained LLM is
prohibitive on consumer hardware; how can we adapt the model
with a small number of additional parameters and match full
fine-tuning performance?


### Proposed method
We propose HyperZero-PEFT, a forward-only, hypernetwork-driven adapter scheme that adapts a frozen LLM using a tiny number of additional parameters while matching full fine-tuning. Insert lightweight, parallel adapters in the FFN (LoRA-style down/up projections) and short learned key/value prefixes in attention. A single shared hypernetwork generates all adapter and prefix weights for every layer from compact inputs that concatenate a task embedding, a layer-id embedding, and a block-type embedding. The generated modules are injected with residual scaling and per-layer learned gates to softly select where adaptation matters most. Only the hypernetwork, small embedding tables, the per-layer gates, and optionally layer norms are trained; the LLM weights remain frozen.

Training is performed with a two-point zeroth-order (ZO) estimator on the hypernetwork parameters, eliminating backpropagation through the base LLM so memory stays near inference. At each step, draw an antithetic perturbation for the hypernetwork weights, run two forward passes to compute a finite-difference loss, and update with an Adam-like optimizer. We deterministically resample perturbations by seed to reduce variance and enable gradient accumulation without extra memory. Because the hypernetwork is small and the base model is frozen, a single consumer GPU (e.g., 8–24 GB) can train billions-parameter LLMs with near-inference memory and throughput.

To maximize accuracy under a tiny parameter budget, we add a short sensitivity warm-up that allocates rank and prefix length where they help most. Using a small held-out set, we probe each layer by perturbing its hyper-embedding and measuring loss change, then allocate higher LoRA rank and/or longer prefixes to the top-k most sensitive layers while shrinking others to minimal or zero rank. The hypernetwork learns to specialize by layer and block type, amortizing parameters across the whole stack and across tasks via the task embedding.

At inference, the hypernetwork generates per-layer adapter/prefix weights on the fly or caches them after the first use; switching tasks reduces to swapping a task embedding. The method is compatible with quantized bases (e.g., 4/8-bit) since only small-side modules are trained and applied in higher precision. With typical settings (LoRA rank 4–16; prefix length 8–16; hypernetwork at 0.2%–0.6% of base), HyperZero-PEFT achieves performance on par with full fine-tuning while training and storing orders of magnitude fewer parameters.

### Rationale
HyperZero-PEFT combines two complementary ideas: (a) a shared hypernetwork that amortizes the cost of generating effective adapters and attention prefixes across all layers and tasks, and (b) forward-only zeroth-order training so adaptation fits on consumer hardware. Prior work shows that hypernetworks conditioned on task/layer/block embeddings can produce both adapter and prefix weights that rival or beat standard PEFT while training a tiny fraction of parameters, suggesting strong capacity-per-parameter. ZO optimization, in turn, matches the memory footprint of inference by avoiding backpropagation, and has been shown to scale to large LLMs on a single 24 GB GPU with competitive convergence.

The sensitivity-based budget allocator exploits the observed low effective curvature rank in LLMs to place rank and prefix length where they matter, letting a very small parameter budget approximate the function class of full fine-tuning. Together, these components deliver near-FT accuracy while training and storing only a small, task-portable hypernetwork and embeddings—precisely the constraints required for consumer devices.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Scaling pretrained language models to many tasks with minimal tunable parameters remains challenging: adapter- and prefix-tuning still introduce sizable per-layer/block parameters and are usually trained per task.
   method:  Use a single shared hypernetwork conditioned on hyper-embeddings that encode task, layer-id, and block-type to generate all weights for both prefix-tuning (HyperPrefix) and adapter-tuning (HyperPELT) across the T5 encoder–decoder. For prefixes, expand the hyper-embedding to the prefix length and project it directly to prefix key/value vectors, eliminating the per-block embedding matrix and MLP of standard prefix-tuning; for adapters, generate the down/up projection weights for adapters inserted in parallel to the FFN with a learnable scaling. Only the hypernetwork, input embeddings (task/layer/block), and layer norms are trained; PLM weights are frozen. On GLUE multi-tasking, this cuts trained parameters per task by about 11× vs Prefix-tuning while improving average performance (e.g., HyperPELT 87.09 with 0.24% trained params/task).

2. [LLM_NLP] DPZero: Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models without Backpropagation (2310.09639)
   problem: In practice, DP first-order fine-tuning adds substantial memory/runtime overhead (e.g., per-sample gradient clipping), limiting DP adaptation of very large LLMs on commodity GPUs.
   method:  DPZero performs per-sample clipping on a scalar finite-difference computed with forward passes and injects scalar Gaussian noise, avoiding backprop and per-sample gradient computations (Remark 4.5, lines 205, 209). Experiments show near-zero extra memory/time versus non-private MeZO when fine-tuning RoBERTa (e.g., 2668 MiB and 0.347 s/iter vs MeZO’s 2668 MiB and 0.345 s/iter; DP-AdamW uses 17126 MiB and 2.12 s/iter; Table 3, lines 249–259) and scalability to OPT-6.7B on a single 24 GiB GPU where first-order methods OOM (Table 11, lines 582–589). Utility drops are small under DP (e.g., ≤0.9% at ε=6 on OPT tasks; lines 262–284), validating practical scalability with strong privacy.

3. [LLM_NLP] On the Convergence of Zeroth-Order Federated Tuning for Large Language Models (2402.05926)
   problem: Federated fine-tuning of large language models on resource-limited clients is hindered by the high memory cost of backpropagation and heavy communication, and the convergence behavior of zeroth-order optimization in federated LLM tuning is not established.
   method:  Propose FedMeZO, which integrates the MeZO two-point zeroth-order gradient estimator into FedAvg to eliminate backpropagation, resampling perturbations in-place via random seeds so training memory is comparable to inference, and uses LoRA to update/upload only low-rank adapters to cut communication. The paper derives convergence guarantees that exploit the low effective Hessian rank r of LLMs, proving global rates O(r^{3/2}(NHT)^{-1/2}) in i.i.d. data and O(r^{3/2}((c_h+N)NHT)^{-1/2}) minus a heterogeneity term in non-i.i.d. settings, showing dependence on r rather than the full parameter dimension d. Experiments on LLaMA-based tuning confirm faster loss reduction than BP-based FedAvg with roughly half the GPU memory footprint.

4. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Equipping text-pretrained LMs with vision-and-language capabilities efficiently—without appending image tokens that consume input length or full-model fine-tuning—requires a new mechanism to inject visual information with few parameters.
   method:  VL-HyperPELT treats image features as inputs to hypernetworks rather than as sequence tokens: CLIP-extracted visual features are projected into visual hyper-embeddings, which drive visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific prefix vectors for attention and adapter weights for FFN. These are combined with task-specific outputs (concatenating prefix vectors; parallel adapters) to modulate the frozen T5 while preserving the original text input length. This approach achieves comparable or better performance than VL-Adapter with fewer trainable parameters (6.62% vs 7.16%) and yields stronger few-shot transfer on OKVQA and SNLI-VE.

5. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Playing Lottery Tickets with Vision and Language (2104.11832)
   problem: Large vision-and-language (VLP) models (e.g., UNITER) are over-parameterized, making them costly to deploy; it is unknown whether sparse, trainable subnetworks (lottery tickets) exist that retain performance across diverse VL tasks.
   method:  Conduct the first empirical study of the lottery ticket hypothesis in VLP by applying Iterative Magnitude-based Pruning (IMP) to pre-trained UNITER and re-training from the pre-trained initialization. Across 7 VL tasks (VQA, GQA, VCR, NLVR2, VE, RefCOCO+, Flickr30k IR/TR), identify 'relaxed' winning tickets that maintain ≥99% of full-model accuracy at 50–70% sparsity (e.g., VQA/GQA/RefCOCO+ at 70%, NLVR2/VE/ITR at 60%, VCR at 50%). Show that learned masks are crucial (random pruning underperforms) and that initialization matters (BERT init < UNITER init; shuffled init fails), while rewinding offers little benefit in this setting.

7. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

8. [MULTIMODAL] Bridging Vision and Language Encoders: Parameter-Efficient Tuning for Referring Image Segmentation (2307.11545)
   problem: Referring image segmentation with pre-trained dual-encoder vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) needs strong cross-modal interaction and spatial inductive biases, but full fine-tuning is costly; keeping backbones frozen typically hurts performance.
   method:  Introduce Bridger, an adapter-like module inserted at intermediate stages of frozen image and text encoders to enable early, bidirectional interaction while injecting vision-specific inductive biases. Bridger comprises: (1) a Zoom Layer that reshapes visual features with convolution/deconvolution (for ViT and ResNet) to capture local spatial priors and control sequence length; and (2) an Interactor that performs cross-modal attention using one modality as query and the other as keys/values, then projects and residually adds outputs back into the next backbone stage. This design updates only 1.61%–3.38% of backbone parameters yet matches or surpasses full fine-tuning; ablations show removing Bridger drops oIoU by ~3.33%.

9. [CV] Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models (2401.08541)
   problem: Next-patch pixel prediction can force the backbone to specialize in low-level details, hurting the quality of transferable features.
   method:  Insert a heavily-parameterized token-level MLP prediction head atop the ViT trunk that processes each patch independently before projecting to pixels. This head captures low-level signals needed for accurate pixel regression, keeping the trunk focused on higher-level, transferable representations. Experiments show the MLP head significantly improves downstream performance over having no head, a transformer head brings only marginal gains at much higher cost, and increasing head depth (more MLP blocks) is especially beneficial.

   method:  Propose a Conservative Offline Policy Estimator that performs fitted Q evaluation augmented with a CQL-style regularizer to penalize Q-values for OOD actions, producing a lower-bound estimate of a policy’s per-session long-term value from held-out logs. This estimator guides offline model selection and aligns with observed online outcomes, filtering policies that would degrade user experience.

11. [RL] Reward-Weighted Regression Converges to a Global Optimum (2107.09088)

   method:  Derive a closed-form RWR policy update (no function approximation) expressed via value functions: define B(π) with π_{n+1}(a|s) = (Q^{π_n}(s,a)/V^{π_n}(s)) · π_n(a|s). Prove this induces monotonic improvement of both V^{π} and Q^{π} at every state-action (Theorem 4.1), with strict improvement whenever Var_{a∼π_n(·|s)}[Q^{π_n}(s,a)]>0. Show that nonlinear reward transformations can change the optimal policy (Appendix A), so restrict to linear shifts yielding strictly positive rewards to ground the update and analysis (Theorem 3.1).

12. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

After search, it performs selective retraining to convergence for each candidate and selects the best-performing mask; the scheme can be extended to additional lower sparsities to obtain optimal masks for multiple memory budgets in one run.

For each query, aggregate N×G scores from all documents and rollouts, sort them, and compute rewards that: (a) give positives high reciprocal-rank rewards, (b) penalize negatives ranked above any positive, and (c) give correctly-below negatives a smooth squared-error reward against the SFT reference score. Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. 17. [SPEECH] Application-Agnostic Language Modeling for On-Device ASR (2305.09764)

   method:  They propose a data balancing scheme that derives per-source sampling weights for a single, application-agnostic NNLM. First, build a balanced dev set (≈equal VA/STT). Compute optimal interpolation weights that minimize perplexity for application-specific LMs on this dev set to obtain redistribution masses for VA and STT. Use these to rescale initial per-source sampling weights (from count-merging) and renormalize, then sample heldout/train data accordingly.    problem: End-to-end ASR/SLU encoders must capture both local and global dependencies, but popular designs like Conformer combine convolution and self-attention sequentially, making utilization of local/global cues hard to interpret or customize and leaving quadratic attention cost.
   method:  Branchformer introduces a two-branch encoder where one branch uses multi-headed self-attention with relative positional encoding for global context, and the other uses an MLP with convolutional spatial gating (cgMLP) employing depth-wise convolutions for local context. The branches are merged via either concatenation (default, best accuracy) or a learned weighted average whose attention-pooled weights expose layer-wise importance of local vs. global processing. This parallel, disentangled design yields better or comparable results to Conformer across ASR/SLU benchmarks and improves training stability on short-utterance data.

19. [ROBOTICS] Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation (1909.12906)
   problem: On-policy adaptation on real robots is sample-inefficient and risky in high-dimensional, low-level action spaces, leading to unsafe or erratic exploration.
   method:  Train a variational autoencoder on a dataset of safe, task-specific robot trajectories to define a low-dimensional latent action space; the policy outputs latent vectors conditioned on the goal, which are decoded into smooth executable trajectories. This reduces action dimensionality, focuses learning on terminal rewards, and enables faster, safer on-policy adaptation. The learned 2D latent space disentangles control factors (e.g., hitting angle vs. strength), yielding predictable control and stable updates.

20. [ROBOTICS] Causal Reasoning in Simulation for Structure and Transfer Learning of Robot Manipulation Policies (2103.16772)
   problem: Sim-to-real manipulation policies often overfit to large, distractor-laden state spaces, making them brittle under domain shifts and sample-inefficient to adapt. A key unmet need is to identify which context/state variables are causally relevant to a parameterized controller and to exploit this structure for transfer.
   method:  CREST performs simulation-based causal reasoning with an internal (approximate) model to discover the policy’s input structure. Phase 1 intervenes on one context variable at a time (||Δc||0=1) after solving for task-specific controller parameters θi, and marks a variable as relevant if reusing θi fails; this yields the relevant set τ. Phase 2 identifies parameter-specific inputs τj by re-solving under interventions on τ and using a breadth-first search to find a minimal set of parameter changes (min ||Δθ||0), assigning each θj the variables whose interventions necessitated changing θj. The learned structure is encoded in Reduced MLPs (inputs τ) and Partitioned MLPs (per-parameter towers with inputs τj), pretrained in the internal model with domain randomization only over τ, then transferred and fine-tuned in the target domain.

21. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

1. Evolution of meta's llama models and parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models: a survey (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)
   This review surveys the rapid evolution of Meta AI's LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) series - from LLaMA 1 through LLaMA 4 and the specialized parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods developed for these models. We first describe the LLaMA family of foundation models (7B-65B to 288B parameters), their architectures (including native multimodal and Mixtureof-Experts variants), and key performance characteristics. We then describe and discuss the concept of PEFT, which adapts large pre-trained models by updating only a small subset of parameters, and review five PEFT methods that have been applied to LLaMA: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), LLaMA-Adapter V1 and V2, LLaMA-Excitor, and QLoRA (Quantized LoRA). We discuss each method's mechanism, parameter savings, and example application to LLaMA (e.g., instruction tuning, multimodal tasks). We provide structured discussion and analysis of model and adapter architectures, parameter counts, and benchmark results (including examples where fine-tuned LLaMA models outperform larger baselines). Finally, we examine real-world use cases where LLaMA-based models and PEFT have been successfully applied (e.g., legal and medical domains), and we discuss ongoing challenges and future research directions (such as scaling to even larger contexts and improving robustness). This survey paper provides a one-stop resource for ML researchers and practitioners interested in LLaMA models and efficient fine-tuning strategies.

2. MoA: Heterogeneous Mixture of Adapters for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Recent studies integrate Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to further enhance the performance of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods in Large Language Model (LLM) applications. Existing methods employ \emph{homogeneous} MoE-LoRA architectures composed of LoRA experts with either similar or identical structures and capacities. However, these approaches often suffer from representation collapse and expert load imbalance, which negatively impact the potential of LLMs. To address these challenges, we propose a \emph{heterogeneous} \textbf{Mixture-of-Adapters (MoA)} approach. This method dynamically integrates PEFT adapter experts with diverse structures, leveraging their complementary representational capabilities to foster expert specialization, thereby enhancing the effective transfer of pre-trained knowledge to downstream tasks. MoA supports two variants: \textbf{(i)} \textit{Soft MoA} achieves fine-grained integration by performing a weighted fusion of all expert outputs; \textbf{(ii)} \textit{Sparse MoA} activates adapter experts sparsely based on their contribution, achieving this with negligible performance degradation. Experimental results demonstrate that heterogeneous MoA outperforms homogeneous MoE-LoRA methods in both performance and parameter efficiency.    This paper studies the challenging task of Referring Expression Comprehension (REC), which aims at detecting the text-referred target object in an input image. This will limit their effectiveness in REC tasks, where the positional information is essential to correctly localize the target object. To this end, we propose a novel parameter-efficient tuning approach, named Multi-Modal Adaptive Positional Encoding (MAP), which addresses the above problem from a new perspective of positional encoding. More specifically, MAP first generates initial positional embeddings for different visual encoder layers from a set of learnable vectors, and then adjusts them adaptively based on spatial-wise visual-linguistic correlations of input data. In this way, the positional information of different image tokens can be appropriately modeled and utilized by MAP, thus making it more applicable to REC tasks. In summary, CPEL demonstrates robust generalization and cross-domain adaptability, highlighting its scalability and effectiveness as a transformative solution for domain adaptation in specialized, resource-constrained fields.

In contrast, dynamic LoRA introduces a mechanism that dynamically evaluates the importance of layer during fine-tuning. This evaluation enables the reallocation of adapter parameters to fit the unique demands of each individual task, which leads to better optimization results. The joint approach boosts not only the performance over each single task but also the generalization ability of the model. The efficiency of the dynamic LoRA was validated in experiments on benchmark datasets, such as GLUE, with surprising results. More specifically, this method achieved 88.1% accuracy with an F1-score of 87.3%. Noticeably, these improvements were made at a slight increase in computational costs: only 0.1% more resources than standard LoRA. This balance between performance and efficiency positions dynamic LoRA as a practical, scalable solution for fine-tuning LLMs, especially in resource-constrained scenarios. 2. IGU-LoRA: Adaptive Rank Allocation via Integrated Gradients and Uncertainty-Aware Scoring ( 2026, cites: 0)

Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) mitigates this issue by updating only a small set of task-specific parameters while keeping the base model frozen. Among PEFT approaches, low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is widely adopted; however, it enforces a uniform rank across layers despite substantial variation in layer importance, motivating {layerwise} rank allocation. Recent adaptive-rank variants (e.g., AdaLoRA) allocate ranks based on importance scores, yet typically rely on instantaneous gradients that capture only local sensitivity, overlooking non-local, pathwise effects within the same layer, which yields unstable and biased scores. To address this limitation, we introduce IGU-LoRA, an adaptive-rank LoRA that (i) computes within-layer Integrated Gradients (IG) sensitivities and aggregates them into a layer-level score for rank allocation, and (ii) applies an uncertainty-aware scheme using exponential moving averages with deviation tracking to suppress noisy updates and calibrate rank selection. Theoretically, we prove an upper bound on the composite trapezoidal rule approximation error for parameter-space IG under a pathwise Hessian-Lipschitz condition, which informs the quadrature budget. Across diverse tasks and architectures, IGU-LoRA consistently outperforms strong PEFT baselines at matched parameter budgets, improving downstream accuracy and robustness. Ablations confirm the contributions of pathwise within-layer sensitivity estimates and uncertainty-aware selection to effective rank allocation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/withyou12/igulora.git

In the mean time, the outer ZO provides better generalization ability for PEFT. We provide theoretical convergence guarantees and empirically demonstrate that Bilevel-ZOFO significantly outperforms existing ZO and FO-PEFT methods, achieving 2-4 times faster training while maintaining similar memory efficiency. We propose a simple yet effective parameter selection scheme that yields significant performance gains with Sparse-MeZO. Additionally, we develop a memory-optimized implementation for sparse masking, ensuring the algorithm requires only inference-level memory consumption, allowing Sparse-MeZO to fine-tune LLaMA-30b on a single A100 GPU. Experimental results illustrate that Sparse-MeZO consistently improves both performance and convergence speed over MeZO without any overhead. Extensive experiments on OPT and Llama across diverse NLP tasks show that CurvZO consistently improves fine-tuning performance and reduces training time over ZO baselines. Reasoning: The proposed HyperZero-PEFT combines three mechanisms: (1) a shared hypernetwork generating both LoRA adapters and prefix KV weights conditioned on task/layer/block embeddings (directly matching HyperFormer 2021 and HyperPELT 2022, and HyperLoader 2024), (2) zeroth-order (MeZO-style) training applied only to the small hypernetwork parameters to avoid backprop through the frozen LLM, and (3) a sensitivity warm-up that allocates rank/prefix length per layer. The hypernetwork-for-adapters component is well-covered by HyperFormer/HyperPELT/HyperLoader. ZO applied to PEFT adapters is covered by AdaZeta (tensor-train adapter + ZO) and Bilevel ZOFO (ZO + PEFT). However, no surfaced paper combines a shared hypernetwork (generating all adapter+prefix weights from task/layer/block embeddings) with ZO optimization of the hypernetwork parameters specifically. The closest is AdaZeta (ZO + small adapter, no hypernetwork) and HyperLoader (hypernetwork + adapters, no ZO). This matches the Score 4 anchor: combining mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, with searches returning prior work on individual mechanisms but not their combination.

Prior works:
  - Parameter-efficient Multi-task Fine-tuning for Transformers via Shared Hypernetworks / HyperFormer (2021): shared hypernetwork conditioned on task/layer/adapter-position generates adapter weights for all layers — directly covers the hypernetwork component
  - HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning (2022): hypernetwork generates both prefix and adapter weights from task/layer/block embeddings — covers the combined prefix+adapter generation via hypernetwork
  - HyperLoader: Integrating Hypernetwork-Based LoRA and Adapter Layers into Multi-Task Transformers (2024): hypernetwork generates LoRA and adapter weights conditioned on task/layer/position — covers LoRA-style hypernetwork generation
  - AdaZeta: Adaptive Zeroth-Order Tensor-Train Adaption for Memory-Efficient LLM Fine-Tuning (2024): ZO optimization applied to a small adapter module (tensor-train) for memory-efficient LLM fine-tuning — covers ZO+small-adapter combination
  - Bilevel ZOFO: Bridging Parameter-Efficient and Zeroth-Order Techniques (2025): bilevel method coupling ZO full-model updates with FO-PEFT inner loop — covers ZO+PEFT combination but not hypernetwork-generated adapters

messages.content:
You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Updating billions of parameters to adapt a pretrained LLM is
prohibitive on consumer hardware; how can we adapt the model
with a small number of additional parameters and match full
fine-tuning performance?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed 2112b9511767 [IR_REC] — "GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
- Method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

### Seed a9647aa8fe5e [IR_REC] — "Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
- Method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

### Seed 3917da5fd88a [LLM_NLP] — "Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
- Method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

### Seed 663d598bb0b9 [CV] — "KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
- Method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

### Seed 6bafdc8c3e0c [ROBOTICS] — "Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
- Method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

### Seed 9afe61a7b42b [ROBOTICS] — "Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Updating billions of parameters to adapt a pretrained LLM is
prohibitive on consumer hardware; how can we adapt the model
with a small number of additional parameters and match full
fine-tuning performance?


### Proposed method
We propose Sparse Global–Local Adapters (SGLA), a parameter‑efficient adaptation scheme that adds fewer than ~0.5–1.0% new parameters to a frozen pretrained LLM while approaching full fine‑tuning performance. SGLA combines two complementary components: (1) cross‑layer global conditioning via lightweight learned prefixes that inject a task/domain embedding into the attention stacks of selected layers, and (2) a tiny bank of low‑rank adapters per selected layer with prototype‑routed gating so the model can express task‑specific transformations without touching the base weights.

Layer placement is selected automatically with a probe‑and‑prune procedure. First, we run the frozen LLM and attach layer‑wise linear probes to measure which layers most correlate with downstream task signal on a small validation set. We then initialize small scalar gates per candidate layer and train under a group‑sparsity regularizer so only the few most useful layers remain active. We place SGLA modules only in these layers (typically 4–8 late layers on decoder models), sharply reducing the parameter budget while targeting the layers where task knowledge concentrates.

Within each selected layer, we (a) prepend a short, learned key/value prefix (e.g., 8–32 virtual tokens) that acts as a global task attribute, injected into the attention computation across the forward pass; and (b) attach a tiny bank (e.g., 2–4) of low‑rank adapters to the attention and MLP projections with very small ranks (r≈4–8). A lightweight routing head matches the current hidden state to a handful of learned prototypes (cosine similarity), producing mixture weights that softly select which adapter(s) to engage for the token or sequence. This yields mixture‑of‑adapters expressivity with minimal added parameters and compute. The routing head also outputs a calibrated confidence signal that we use to schedule updates.

Training proceeds in two short phases on consumer hardware. Phase A (stabilize and align): we distill token‑level logits from a reference model (e.g., a fully fine‑tuned or larger teacher when available) and adversarially align intermediate representations between the adapted and frozen bases to preserve general capability. The prefix vectors are updated with a conservative, uncertainty‑guided EMA: examples the router deems uncertain get larger prompt updates, while certain ones get smaller nudges. Phase B (task specialization): we optimize the task objective (e.g., SFT or preference learning) only over the SGLA parameters. We use a loss‑adaptive momentum optimizer that estimates noise online from the scalar loss and gradients, stabilizing training with small batches and reducing hyperparameter sensitivity typical of consumer GPUs. Throughout, only the prefixes, adapter banks, routers, and layer gates are trainable; the base model remains frozen.

At inference, the model loads just the selected‑layer prefixes, adapter banks, and tiny router. For multi‑task or continual setups, we maintain one prefix per task/domain and share the adapter banks; swapping prefixes selects the behavior with negligible overhead. The small, routed adapter design keeps memory and latency close to the frozen base while delivering accuracy comparable to full fine‑tuning on many instruction‑following and domain adaptation workloads.

### Rationale
Targeting late layers concentrates adaptation capacity where decoder backbones encode task‑specific behavior, allowing a tiny number of parameters to effect large changes. Cross‑layer global prefixes act like a task attribute that conditions attention everywhere it matters, while the local low‑rank adapters provide just‑enough capacity to reshape computations. Prototype‑routed mixtures increase expressivity under the same budget and supply an uncertainty signal we can exploit to allocate updates and prevent overfitting.

Uncertainty‑guided prompt updates and adversarial feature alignment preserve generalization and mitigate catastrophic forgetting when training with small batches on consumer hardware. Finally, a loss‑adaptive, momentum‑aware optimizer with online noise estimation stabilizes the small set of trainable parameters without expensive tuning. Together, these ingredients allow SGLA to approach or match full fine‑tuning performance for many tasks while adding orders‑of‑magnitude fewer parameters and fitting comfortably on commodity GPUs.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)
   problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
   method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).
